entries.text: All right. Hi, everybody. Welcome. My name is Arie Edmondson. I'm a lecturer here in the History department. I teach in the Human Context and Ethics program. Some of you might know me from Data 104, if you don't already. There's a good chance you'll see me again in the future in that class. So I'm here today to talk to you about.
Well, a story, a case study that's going to be at the center of your upcoming upcoming project A.

It's a story about something called the Cook County Assessor's Office, or CCAO for short, as I'll be referring to it, and their Open Data Initiative. It's a case study itself is the source of the data you're about to work with on this upcoming project. And it's a story about what's involved in trying to use some of the tools of data science that you're learning about in this class to try and solve real world problems, to do real world kind of political work.

And so my presentation of this case study will help you kind of have some context for thinking about what's at stake and how to reason more broadly and contextually about what you're going to be doing in your project A, where you, you know, build a model and predict housing prices.
So what's this case study about? Well, let's start with the place. Anybody know where Cook County is? This place called Cook County? I see hand coming up. Where is it?
OK. So you know, are you from there?

But you know a lot about Cook County. Yeah. So it's it is it's it is in Illinois. It's basically Chicago and Chicago sort of surrounding townships borders right on Illinois and on Lake, MI there. And So what you see here is this image of Cook County, Illinois. And it's about this institution called an assessor's office. This is an office that is tasked with doing something that, you know, probably to you is, you know, most of you are in your, you know, early 20s or teens and.

Probably going to sound really dry and boring, but it's about assessing property values and doing this at scale. That is, it's about it's an agency in the county that's tasked with figuring out how much every residential and commercial property is worth in the county and doing this every three years.
And this is a difficult task.
They can't just figure out what this is based on its last sale price, because if something hasn't sold in decades.
It's no longer.

That that that price from three decades ago doesn't really mean a lot about its value today. Why does an assessor's office need to do something like this? Assess property values at a regular interval? Anybody have a guess or no?
Yeah.

Yeah, so Illinois and most states in the US assess property taxes based on an estimated current value of a home. Now, California pioneered something that exists in a handful of other states back in the 70s with Prop 13, which means your property taxes are pegged to its original sale value, and they can only go up a small amount. Parts of problems in California State budgets are related to this.
Which we're not going to get into today, but it might be a strange thing if you're from California, because.

Our assessors don't have to worry about making these estimates. There's just a kind of fixed small amount, something that can go up. So if you bought your house in the 1970s, your property taxes are extremely low compared to somebody who just bought their similar home down the street the previous year.

So doing this, figuring out how do we estimate the value of a home is a very data intensive practice. It's a massive operation in part just because of the scale of the of properties that have to be assessed, especially in a county like Cook County where you have about 900,000 assessed properties in in in a cycle, more or less.

So today what I'm going to do is I'm going to walk you through a case study related to this institution and why it has to do what it does and what sorts of problems it's encountered. And I'm going to do this in a few stages. We're going to focus at first on just like, what was the problem? What was the problem that confronted this agency such that it turned to a new kind of data intensive system for trying to assess property values? What is the political social problem underneath all of this?
How did they try to mobilize data to solve this problem subsequently, and we're going to spend the bulk of our time.

Doing that work of tracing out the response and, and, and giving you some tools to analyze how this is a very political act. And then at the very end, I'll just leave you with a few key takeaways about like, what's the point of approaching a case study like this in, in the ways that we're, we're going to walk through today.

So let's start with the problem, the situation that prompted the Cook County Assessor's Office to form a sort of new open data initiative internal to itself, one that's now actually migrated and central to like all the county offices. But it's one that kind of made the situation where you got the data that made the data open. It's there for you to use in this project. Where did that come about? Well, it started with a scandal, a scandal that goes back to this 2017 investigative report from the Chicago Tribune.

And they basically uncovered a major scandal in the property assessment system managed by the CCAO under the watch of then County Assessor Joseph Barrios. So the every assessor's office is headed by a county assessor who's actually democratically elected by the citizens in the county. Now, journalists working for the Chicago Tribune, working with experts from the University of Chicago.
Investigated and then demonstrated.
The presence of systematic bias and corruption that was resulting in a highly regressive tax system whose burden felt disproportionately on poor and non white homeowners in Cook County.

So the journalists took advantage of data analysis and visualization techniques to try to make visible to a public the workings of this unjust system and to try to make the argument that it was in fact unjust and explain why they think this happened.

Now they referenced some standard metrics that are used in the assessment business, which we'll refer to a little bit later, and convincingly argued that the system not only was inaccurate, that is the assessments it's producing were error ridden, but that the assessments were inaccurate in a way that made them unfair systematically.

In other words, the errors, the distribution of errors, converted what's supposed to be a flat tax rate into an effectively regressive tax rate. What do I mean by that? Some of you know this, some might not. So property taxes are supposed to be a flat tax rate. No matter how much your income, whatever your income is, no matter how much your home is worth, you should be taxed the same percentage, sort of, no matter what.
How they set the percentage is based on the total aggregate amount they expect to collect.
But they set that rate and it's supposed to be the same for everybody. You get the number and you just multiply the value of the home by that, you know, .08% or whatever.
A regressive tax rate of course is 1, where the more income you have, or in this case the more your home is worth, the lower the effective tax rate is. So the wealthier you are, the less you pay.
Proportionally and progressive is the opposite. Progressive tax is 1. Whether in in practice or by rule, you tax people more the more wealthy they are.

So this is supposed to be a flat tax rate in practice. Basically what they demonstrated was that the property tax rate in Cook County was effectively regressive according to a number of different metrics.

So on the right over here you have a graph illustrating the regressivity of the effect of property tax rate with respect not just to property values but to median household incomes. Because there is I think you would expect to see a correlation between household income and the value of homes. It's not a one simple correlation, but but more or less does. So the the the wealthy you were, the less effectively you were paying in taxes proportionally.
So the system was regressive, in other words, because it distributed tax burdens unevenly and in a particular direction.
Placing A disproportionate burden on lower income homeowners.

Now, some of this, people have pointed out, could have been due to the effects of the recession. This is data, you know, from that shows what was happening in the 20 tens roughly, that perhaps it was because there was a sudden dip and then rise in home prices that you have this kind of inaccuracy. But it was noted in comparison with other counties that you didn't have the same drastic changes in these metrics. So they realized something else was going on that was more specific to Cook County.
One thing that they also noticed, and this was due in part to the fact that.

Cook County and Chicago is, according to a number of metrics, regularly boasts of being one of the most segregated, perhaps in some metrics the most segregated city in the United States. This discrepancy, because it mapped onto class, also mapped very clearly on to race as well. So the regressivity might have also been legally discriminatory because of the way the burden fell disproportionately.
On Cook County's nonwhite homeowners.

OK. Just a quick sort of check your understanding, Paul, because I know sometimes when looking at this project, students will make a mistake about this. If you're a home owner and you are thinking about tax assessors valuing your home, do you want the tax assessor to, is it in your interest, in your material interest to have your home undervalued or to have your home overvalued?
Let's give you all a second to think about it and.
Post your answers.
OK.

So it looks like the bulk of you are answering get this right, at least right in the way that I'm hoping you understand this. If you're a homeowner, you might want your home to be highly valued on the market. You know you get more money when you sell it, but that when you have a tax assessor assessing the value of your home, the higher it's priced, the more you pay. So generally, unless you're somebody that just like loves to pay taxes.
You usually will want your tax to be undervalued or low. You know the lower the.
Value the tax assessor thinks it is the better four year taxes.

OK, so that's a that's a kind of just crucial thing to keep in mind about the interests at work here. Let's run one more poll. Which of the following is an example of a regressive tax? Just to make sure we got our definitions of what regressive taxation means. Is it that an owner of an inexpensive home pays a higher tax rate than owners of expensive homes, or vice versa? The other other side, owners of expensive homes pay a higher tax rate than owners of inexpensive homes?
OK, looks like the vast majority of you got this one right too. Great job. We know what regressivity is.
OK.
And as some of you might still be trickling in with your answers, so I'll give you a few more seconds before I move on.
OK.

So it's clear that there was some sort of a problem. The next step in all of this was to figure out what actually caused it. Now, if you're familiar with some conventional data ethics case studies, you might think that I'm here to teach you about algorithmic bias. There was an algorithm, it was biased, and they found a way to fix it. The thing is, that's not really the story here, or at least it's not really the main one that I'm here to talk to you about. It wasn't primarily a technical issue of, you know, poor modeling of a bad.

The model, that being said, that might have been a piece of what the story was very simple models like linear regression that they did use during Berios tenure do tend to be regressive because they regress towards the mean price. But in large part it was something else really that that caused a lot of this divergent. It was in fact it was because of a social structure, the institutional social structure of the property assessment system in which a human review process enabled those who are wealthy.
And more privileged to more easily and successfully challenge their assessments.

How does this work? So at first glance, this seems like something good and intuitive. You get mailed this assessment. It's a kind of highly automated process that generates some number because it has to be done at scale. You get it in the mail and you look at it and you might be like, this has got to be wrong. Like, I know what I bought my house for. I see this number.

There must be some way I can go and get this adjusted right. And so indeed there is a whole tax review board and you can go and you can challenge your assessment, go before real human beings and making your case.

And argue, oh, you've overvalued my home, I deserve to pay less in taxes. And this exists. And it sounds like a good important idea you have. Everybody has same equal access to this board. But the problem is, is that equal access is not actually as equal in practice as it sounds. In theory. Everybody legally can go to this board, But to actually have the material ability to go to that board and make a convincing case is another story.

Because knowing the intricacies of the tax code and intricacies of like, what's a good argument for demonstrating that an error has been made requires a certain level of expertise that most of us don't have. So what do you do? You hire a tax lawyer.

And that costs money. And indeed, in Chicago, there was a massive and thriving tax attorney industry largely dedicated to helping wealthy people go and convince the tax board to get their people wealthy people's taxes lowered. And this is something that's just not affordable or accessible to most lower income Chicagoans.

And So what you have in practice is that wealthier and wider neighborhoods would appeal more often and when reductions far more often than their less wealthy neighbors. In 2015 alone, residential property owners filed assessment appeals involving 370,000 properties winning reductions 80% of the time. So this was happening at a large scale. A lot of people were making a lot of money off of it, and wealthy homeowners were was worth it to them. They were paying so much less in property taxes, it was worth paying.
A lawyer.
To help them get their assessment lowered.

Now, the Tribune's analysis of this pointed towards corruption, and a lack of transparency is the root of this problem. Barrios, the county assessor, had really close ties to the tax lawyer industry. In fact, more than half of his campaign contributions came from tax law firms that benefited from this setup.
The chief partner of the biggest law firm dealing with the largest property appeals was in fact the Illinois House Speaker.
And in fact, industry plays a really powerful role and still does today in Illinois and Chicago politics.

And one of the things that you see here was a clever adaptation of this language of community engagement, of keeping the human in the loop to give the system a veneer of fairness, even where that very institution was the cause of this unfairness.

Now, as the Tribune reported, a large number of black and Latino homeowners purchased affordable houses one year, only to find their house appraised at levels far in excess of what they paid for it, suddenly threatening them with the possibility of no longer being able to afford their home.

Right. The thing to think about this is not just an inconvenience. Some people are paying a little more, Some or not people staked their entire future and the money that they have in taking out a mortgage based on the expectation of being able to pay a certain rate. But when suddenly you're overvalued, you're having to pay more in taxes, that puts you in a really difficult situation.

Because you can potentially lose your house, have it foreclosed on because you can't make your mortgage payments, or you have to sell it because you can no longer afford once you're suddenly responsible for paying thousands of extra dollars a year and more than you had budgeted.

Now, even if your home is not valued, or let's just say overvalued, if wealthy homeowners win this disproportionate number of reductions, poor homeowners actually end up paying more as a result. It's a kind of complicated process, but basically the county sets a fixed amount that they're going to collect and they set the tax rate based on it. And so if wealthy people are overall being assessed as having a smaller proportion of the total value.

Of all residential properties, then effectively the lower income people, even if their houses are being accurately assessed, end up paying more proportionally than everyone else because of that sort of 0 sum setup.
Came to be as segregated as it was.

Now, not only is this unfair in all these ways, but the issues of justice goes deeper here, in part because of the ways that this replicated those much older patterns of racially discriminatory housing practices in Chicago that go back decades, and a lot of early and very racialized real estate practices going back to things like redlining that you may have learned about elsewhere. Many of those were indeed pioneered in Chicago and were part of the story of how Chicago.
OK, let's do a quick poll.

So just to check our understanding, what was identified as the primary cause of this unfair burden in the Cook County property assessments in the 20 tens? And so you have a few options. There was racial bias in the data, which caused the model to overvalue the homes of black homeowners. The CCAO use a simple linear model, which tends to be regressive. The Board of Review disproportionately favored wealthier homeowners, reducing their relative tax burden and increasing that of poor homeowners. And then the last.
The CCO made it difficult for poor people to secure mortgages to purchase new homes.
I guess I have to push.
Next to see how you all are voting.
OK.

So there were two correct answers here, both, you know, you could vote multiple times and those ones are in green. Now, again, it's a common story to say that there's racial bias in the day. But as I said, there wasn't actually any clear evidence of racial bias in the data. If one thing people often look for when they look at this is to be like, oh, there's racial bias in the data, It's because there's, you know, race shows up in the data and that undervalues homes. But if some sort of racial devaluations, which take.

And other domains came through in the data that would actually benefit a low income homeowners instead. So that wasn't the primary thing. It had an effect, a racial effect because of the overlapping of class and racial demographics. But racial bias in the data wasn't the main cause. It was these these two top ones.

OK, so we've finished with the problem. Let's talk about what was done and how data was mobilized and leveraged to try to address that and solve this problem. And that'll occupy us from most of the rest of today's lecture.
So what happened was Chicagoans were understandably outraged about this.
And so they elected a new county assessor, a guy named Fritz Kagi, and he had a mandate to fix this problem. And part of the platform he ran on was to create a new Office of Data Science.

To make it open and transparent to the public, to show like here, we're going to publish all our data, we'll talk about our models, we'll make public and transparent our pipeline so everybody can see exactly what we're doing and hold us accountable.

So the idea was not only to create a more accurate algorithmic model, but to design a whole kind of socio technical system to try to rectify these problems that had been identified in the prior administration.

Now what I'm going to do is sort of walk through how the Cook County Assessors office and their data science department tried to develop their solution by reconstructing and analyzing their path through the kind of classic data science life cycle that you've touched on. In this class. I'll be focusing in particular on how you frame and understand your task, like what is, you know, looking at the asking a question, framing a problem.
And then thinking about how your understanding of the context.

Like the meaning of your very text task shapes how you frame the problem in ways that affect how you create a model and assess and evaluate your model at the end, which will all be things that you'll be asked to do in in your project.
OK, so you're a data scientist, you're working for the CCAO to build this new system, and you want to replace this old and unfair 1?

Now, the way that the problem was framed shapes the task you have before you. And in some ways the problem was already framed for you by the Chicago Tribune story and was framed for you by this county assessor. The CCAO has lost public trust, and it's trying to restore that trust. The primary problem is not about data. It's not about accuracy. It's not about error. It's errors. It's about social trust, and it's about trying to mobilize data science to restore trust.
The CCAO and the public seem to agree.
That the loss of trust has to do with the unfairness of the system.

Now for the CCAO at least, the system, the unfairness of the system as a result of this other concept, accuracy, it was unfair because it was inaccurate, and not just inaccurate in a generic way, but inaccurate in a particular way, right? That overburdened those with the fewest means. So their task was to create a system that would be more accurate and therefore more fair, and thus to restore trust in the agency.
As they put it, the task was to, quote, create a robust pipeline that accurately assesses property values at scale and is fair.
So there's some assumptions here.
What is public trust about? In this case? It's a matter of this thing called fairness.
Fairness, in turn, is a matter of accuracy. An accuracy, in turn, has to do with a certain way of measuring error.

So as we go forward, I'm going to put some pressure on this, but for now, just note that there is a whole series of equivalences between different concepts that are made to seem to be identical. Part of what the work they're doing is naturalizing the sense that public trust is about fairness, is about accuracy, is about error.

OK, real quick, so which of the following best describes the goal of the CCIO and its new data science department? Is it? I'm sorry this is hard to read because it keeps changing order. Restore public trust in the agency. Ensure distributional equity and property taxes by maintaining a flat tax rate. Undermine the ability of the wealthy homeowners to disproportionately win appeals at the Board of review. Create a robust pipeline that estimates the value of each property.
Accurately, uniformly, and impartially.
OK.

Most of you say restore public trust, so there's no right or wrong answer here. In a way, all of these are true. Which of the following, you know, best describes the goal? That's an interpretive thing. All of these in some way describe the goal. But you'd say the highest level goal is restoring public trust. And each of these is sort of a translation of like, what does it mean to do that? It means to do this whole series of other things.
So one quick question is, do you think that the goals on the previous slide are all equivalent to one another or do they mean different things?

OK, so they've convinced most of you that indeed all of those different goals actually mean the same thing. If you do any one of them, you're kind of doing the others. So they're radically different concepts in terms that most of you feel convinced by this series of equivalences, a series of translations from one one goal to another, restore public trust in the CCIO, ensure distributional equity and property taxes, etcetera, all the way down to, you know, this more technical.
Of creating, you know, a robust pipeline that estimates the value of each property accurately, uniformly, and impartially.
Nope. What I want to suggest to you is to not take for granted that these all mean the same thing.
That there is a very social goal that takes a detour through several different layers of kinds of technical practices.

And this detour is in some way supposed to go and actually solve the problem, that by translating one problem into another into another, you somehow make it more tractable, and that allows you to go back and actually do the original thing. So part of what we're studying is how this detour through the abstractions of data and algorithms can actually be used to address this problem. Part of it is by convincing people that all of these things are identical, despite in some ways they're very obvious semantic differences.
OK.

So if the technical goal we have at hand is to create a robust pipeline that accurately assesses property values at scale, what does it actually mean to do that? What role does this concept of scale play? In what sense is making accurate property assessments a task for a data scientist? So in some sense, there's three questions here that we'll be working through kind of very deliberately and carefully. What exactly is an assessment, A single assessment? What does it mean? What is being assessed?

We all think we know what that means, but I think it turns out to be surprisingly tricky. What does it mean for any assessments to be accurate? And what makes an accurate assessment not just accurate, but fair? And we'll break this down one step at a time.
So let's start with that very simple question. What is an assessment? So we know at some level in assessment, we've already gone through this. It's the estimate of a value of a home.
And what kind of a value? Well, obviously we've already established its monetary value, it's how much it's worth in dollar terms.

Now my question, though, is what determines the dollar value of a home? There's a lot of different ways that you could imagine kind of guessing at the economic value of a home if you don't actually have recent, if it wasn't sold, say in the last few days, you could kind of look at it, think about its construction materials and property size. You can imagine a world in which there's a state that just decides what the fair price of a home ought to be given certain.
Features or constraints?

In the past, it was very much up to what a real estate expert thought based on some combination. Their sense of location, materials, quality, size, etcetera, could be based on just whatever a homeowner thinks their property is worth because what they paid for it, what they know about their neighborhood and its properties. But of course, none of these is has much to do with what the property assessors use as their target variable. That is what they're trying to guess and predict.
Of course, what they're trying to predict is its market value.
What it actually sells for, what somebody is willing to pay and someone else is willing to sell at one specific moment in time.
Now this is really important, even though it probably seems really obvious at first glance.

If it's just its market value, which is a single moment, a transaction that takes place at 1 moment in time and not another, it means that a home's value has a hazy kind of existence because market value fluctuates all the time.
This value, this number which you just say is a fact about the world, actually only exists when it's measured. And in this case, the measurement of a home's value just is this market transaction.

So the value of a home, right, is not like a physical object in the world that exists whether or not you measure it. Something that doesn't change its shape when you measure this lectern is a certain height, and its height doesn't depend on me measuring it, though I did just learn that it goes up and down and I can change the height. The value of a home is actually a little different. I could sell my house today for $1,000,000 and whoever buys it could sell it in a few months for $1.2 million without anything fundamental changing about the house.

So what in when between those two sales? What kind of value does a house have? Does it just have no value? Is it the value someone you know somewhere in the middle? Is it just the original sale value? What exactly kind of value does it have?

You could say it has a kind of potential value. It's not valueless. You can take out a mortgage on it based on an assessment of its value. You pay taxes based on this guess, this assessment. So it has a kind of real that those numbers have a reality, but it's market value is indeterminate. And I'd say it's not uncertain. When I say indeterminate, I don't mean uncertain. Like I'm uncertain how tall this lectern is. I'm going to guess if I get out of measuring tape, I can tell you what the real value is. It has a fixed real true value.
That won't change unless somebody.
Pushes this button.
It's indeterminate in the sense that it hasn't been determined yet, and only the act of measuring that is selling it will resolve that indeterminacy.

So to say it's indeterminate doesn't mean that it's simply arbitrary either. Houses don't sell for random or arbitrary prices. If it's a so-called arms length transaction, meaning you know it's a real market transaction. It's not apparent selling their child a house for $5, right? There are reasons why it sells for what it sells for. It can be predictable. There's a structure to the market, right?

So if you're trying to determine the price of a home, that is you're trying to assess its market value for figure out tax burden, the only way of perfectly you could say reliably knowing its value is to actually determine it by selling it.
And in a sense, an estimate is only really accurate if you can test it by selling the property.

Or if you have sales data that you've held out and you can, you know, you'll test your model, it's a different story. And even then, it's only perfectly accurate at that single moment. There's a big recession tomorrow, obviously that number becomes like pretty indeterminate very quickly.
And so, as so many properties haven't been sold for decades, every year that passes makes that previous sale a little bit less reliable as an indicator for what the value is in the present.
And so you could say the resolution of the homes home values indeterminacy at the moment of its sale gets full determinacy the moment of sale that gradually wears off over time.

OK, real quick to check your understanding. When I say the monetary value of a home that hasn't been sold in 10 years, is it the value uncertain? Is it simply unknown? Is it something you cannot determine, it's unable to be determined? Is it nonexistent or is it 0?
All right, let's see what people say.
OK.
Most people say uncertain.
So, uh.

Why is uncertain not what I was trying to suggest right now that it's uncertain.

OK, so right here what I'm getting at, uncertain means would mean it has a real value, a fixed value out there in the world that we simply don't know. When we have some amount of uncertainty about what that real value is, an uncertainty we could quantify and measure. When I say it's indeterminate or unable to be determined at that moment even actually, I'd say that language is.

Little. It should be indeterminate rather than unable to be determined because it can be determined by a sale. But the idea is, is that it's indeterminate because it has not yet been determined. You're guessing it. You're not uncertain about the value. The value doesn't have a kind of stable existence for you to be uncertain about in the same way, OK?
Let's keep moving.

So obviously selling your house is not a great way to know property values. If you're a county assessor, you have to make estimates based on the data that you have. And that's where data science steps in, because with the right data about homes and with sale prices on enough other houses, you can probably predict the value of a property with some degree of reliability.

Even if you're not a data scientist, like you know that there's these websites out there, Zillow and Redfin, that give you these estimates of what they think properties are worth and they constantly update them. They of course don't know the value, but they make estimates and they predict what they think the thing would sell for if it's sold that year.
How do they do this? They do something very similar to what county assessors do and what you'll be doing.

They do it with a lot of data, but you can do this even if you don't have lots of data. Imagine, you know, you own. Think about the home maybe you grew up in. Maybe you know how much your home is worth. You know what or how much you know your parents paid for it some number of years ago. You know what? Some houses that are very similar to it just down the road, sold for, right? You have some sense of like, OK, well, that house is pretty similar to mine.
But it has a little bit better of a view, so it probably is worth what we bought ours for last year, but maybe just.
Like a little bit more.

Of course, as a data scientist, you can be precise because you have so much more data about each home and about all of the different homes. And so that's where you turn to next. Start to look at the data and what's in it. What data do we have and what data might we need that we don't have that would help us to make a better estimate of of the value of a home?

So they've got a lot of data, in particular two different data sets. One data set is full of property characteristics. That is things you see here like for every property, like how many full baths does it have, how many fireplaces does it have, does it have a basement, how many bedrooms, things like that. So you have property characteristics data and you have another data set of recent sales data on all homes in the county. Of course, not all homes have been sold recently, but you want to have the data set of at least.
The most recent ones to help train your model.

And So what you try to do is figure out how do these different data points correlate with each other and with sales price. Now, all this data makes it possible to create a number of different models. What you'll be doing is creating a little linear model to predict sale price, that is, the fair market value of unsold properties, by discovering patterns in datasets containing these known sale prices and characteristics of similar and nearby properties, training the model on the data and applying it to all of these properties.

That you don't have recent sales data for. And so you figure out like, OK, I can get a model that looks pretty reliable on the sales data that I have. I can show myself that it's relatively accurate. And then I can extrapolate that all of these other homes that don't have recent sales data, when I make estimates about them, it'll probably be relatively accurate, yes.
Yeah.
